name: Pocong
description: The Pocong, also known as shroud ghost, is a quintessential Indonesian ghost. It represents the soul of a deceased person trapped in their burial shroud. According to Indonesian folklore, after 40 days of someone's death, the ties binding the shroud are untied. If this ritual isn't performed, the soul remains trapped, resulting in the Pocong's creation. Its appearance is terrifying: a white-shrouded figure with a pale or decaying face, often with vacant, staring eyes. Some tales describe it as having a blackened face. Because its feet are bound, it cannot walk normally; instead, it hops or floats eerily, creating an unsettling atmosphere. The Pocong's presence is often indicated by a pungent smell, a sudden drop in temperature, or unexplained sounds. While generally considered harmless, the Pocong is believed to be lost and disoriented, seeking to return to its grave or to communicate a message. It is a powerful symbol of death, the afterlife, and the importance of proper burial rites in Indonesian culture, serving as a reminder to respect the deceased and fulfill traditional obligations. The creature is a recurring motif in Indonesian horror films and literature, solidifying its place in the national psyche as a symbol of the supernatural and the anxieties surrounding death and the unknown.
subtitle: The Bound Soul of the Shroud
appearance: A figure completely shrouded in white burial cloth (kaffan), with only the face visible. The face is often described as pale, decaying, or blackened, with hollow, staring eyes. The feet are bound together, preventing normal walking.
habitat: Graveyards, abandoned houses, dark forests, roadsides, and any place associated with death or the supernatural.
behavior: Lost and disoriented, searching for its grave or seeking to communicate a message. It hops or floats erratically, often appearing unexpectedly. It is generally considered harmless but can induce fear and unease.

keyMyths.title: The Untied Shroud
keyMyths.description: The Pocong is created when the shroud ties are not untied 40 days after death. This leaves the soul trapped and restless, unable to move on.

keyMyths.title: Seeking the Way Back
keyMyths.description: Many believe the Pocong is simply lost and trying to find its way back to its grave. Helping it can release it from its earthly torment.

keyMyths.title: Delivering a Message
keyMyths.description: Sometimes, the Pocong appears to deliver a message from the deceased, often regarding unfinished business or injustices suffered in life.
